title: List Operations
subtitle: Add, remove, and combine list elements
learning_objectives: Add items to a list with .append() and .insert()
Concatenate two lists with the + operator
Use len() to track list size after modifications

sections.heading: Adding Elements
sections.content: .append(item) adds a single item to the end of the list. .insert(index, item) inserts an item at any position, shifting existing elements to the right. Both methods modify the list in place and return None.
sections.code_example:
fruits = ["apple", "banana"]

# append adds to the end
fruits.append("cherry")
print(fruits)          # ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']

# insert at a specific position
fruits.insert(1, "mango")
print(fruits)          # ['apple', 'mango', 'banana', 'cherry']

sections.heading: Removing Elements
sections.content: Python gives you three ways to remove items. .remove(item) finds and removes the first occurrence of the given value. .pop() removes and returns the last item; .pop(i) removes and returns the item at index i. del list[i] deletes the item at index i without returning it.
sections.code_example:
nums = [10, 20, 30, 20, 40]

# remove first occurrence of 20
nums.remove(20)
print(nums)       # [10, 30, 20, 40]

# pop returns and removes last item
last = nums.pop()
print(last)       # 40
print(nums)       # [10, 30, 20]

# del removes by index
del nums[0]
print(nums)       # [30, 20]

sections.heading: Length and Concatenation
sections.content: len(list) returns the current number of elements — useful after modifications. The + operator concatenates two lists into a new list without changing the originals. You can also use += to extend a list in place.
sections.code_example:
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = [4, 5, 6]

# Concatenation produces a new list
c = a + b
print(c)         # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

# Original lists unchanged
print(a)         # [1, 2, 3]

# len after modifications
a.append(99)
print(len(a))    # 4

exercises.title: Grow a List
exercises.prompt: Start with tasks = ["write code", "test code"]. Append "deploy code" to the end, then insert "review code" at index 1. Print the final list.
exercises.starter_code:
tasks = ["write code", "test code"]

# Append "deploy code"

# Insert "review code" at index 1

print(tasks)
exercises.solution:
tasks = ["write code", "test code"]
tasks.append("deploy code")
tasks.insert(1, "review code")
print(tasks)
hints: tasks.append("deploy code") adds to the end
tasks.insert(1, "review code") places the item at position 1

tests.message: Your code should run without any errors

exercises.title: Remove and Pop
exercises.prompt: Given colors = ["red", "blue", "green", "blue"], remove the first occurrence of "blue" using .remove(), then pop the last element and print the popped value. Finally, print the remaining list.
exercises.starter_code:
colors = ["red", "blue", "green", "blue"]

# Remove first occurrence of "blue"

# Pop the last element and print it

# Print the remaining list
exercises.solution:
colors = ["red", "blue", "green", "blue"]
colors.remove("blue")
popped = colors.pop()
print(popped)
print(colors)
hints: colors.pop() removes the last item and returns it — store it in a variable and print it

tests.message: Your code should run without any errors
The popped value 'blue' should be printed
The remaining list should still contain 'red'

exercises.title: Combine Lists
exercises.prompt: Create two lists: evens = [2, 4, 6] and odds = [1, 3, 5]. Concatenate them into a new list called numbers and print it. Then print the length of numbers.
exercises.starter_code:
evens = [2, 4, 6]
odds = [1, 3, 5]

# Concatenate into numbers

# Print numbers

# Print the length of numbers
exercises.solution:
evens = [2, 4, 6]
odds = [1, 3, 5]
numbers = evens + odds
print(numbers)
print(len(numbers))
hints: Use the + operator: numbers = evens + odds
len(numbers) returns the total count of elements

tests.message: Your code should run without any errors
The output should contain 6 (the length of the combined list)
The combined list should contain the value 2

quiz.question: What does my_list.pop(0) do?
options: Removes the last element and returns it
Removes the first element and returns it
Returns the first element without removing it
quiz.explanation: .pop(i) removes and returns the element at the given index. pop(0) therefore removes and returns the element at index 0 — the first element.

quiz.question: After running a = [1, 2]; b = [3, 4]; c = a + b, what is the value of a?
quiz.explanation: The + operator creates a new list and assigns it to c. The original lists a and b are not modified, so a remains [1, 2].

completion_message: Excellent! You can now add, remove, and combine list elements. These mutation methods are some of the most frequently used tools in Python programming.
